facts.claim: Endogenous GLP-1 levels are lower in the follicular phase compared with the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, attributed to slower gastric emptying during the follicular phase.

source.title: The importance of estradiol for body weight regulation in women
source.authors: Vigil P, Meléndez J, Petkovic G, Del Río JP

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Endogenous GLP-1 levels have been shown to be lower in the follicular phase compared with the luteal phase" Section: GLP-1 and estradiol conjugates.

facts.claim: Supra-physiological concentrations of estradiol decrease insulin receptor expression and IRS-1 levels in muscle and adipose tissue, contributing to insulin resistance and sustained hyperinsulinemia.

source.title: The importance of estradiol for body weight regulation in women
source.authors: Vigil P, Meléndez J, Petkovic G, Del Río JP

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Supra-physiological concentrations of estradiol induce a decrease in the expression of insulin receptors" Section: Insulin.

facts.claim: In postmenopausal women, FSH rises in response to ovarian failure and has been associated with menopausal adiposity and hepatic steatosis; high circulating FSH is a major contributor to gonadectomy-induced obesity in mouse models.

source.title: The importance of estradiol for body weight regulation in women
source.authors: Vigil P, Meléndez J, Petkovic G, Del Río JP

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The rise of FSH at menopause in response to ovarian failure has been associated with menopausal adiposity" Section: Gonadotropins and adiposity.

facts.claim: The prevalence of obesity among adolescent girls aged 12-19 years in 2015/2016 was 20.9%, and 3-11% of these obese adolescent girls had PCOS.

facts.claim: Women significantly reduce their food intake during the peri-ovulatory period of the menstrual cycle, mediated by the anorectic action of estradiol acting on the cortex, hypothalamus, and brainstem.

source.title: The importance of estradiol for body weight regulation in women
source.authors: Vigil P, Meléndez J, Petkovic G, Del Río JP

facts.claim: Eating disorders are highly sex differentiated with a female-to-male ratio ranging from 4:1 to 10:1; binge eating and emotional eating are significantly higher during mid-luteal and pre-menstrual phases compared to follicular/ovulatory phases.

facts.claim: Progesterone levels are positively associated with increased binge eating across the menstrual cycle, while physiological estradiol levels are inversely associated with binge eating; abnormally high estradiol is positively associated with binge eating.

facts.claim: In the PRESTO prospective cohort, use of any menstrual cycle tracking app at baseline was associated with a 20% increase in per-cycle fecundability (FR 1.20, 95% CI 1.13-1.28 for selected apps; FR 1.21, 95% CI 1.13-1.30 for other apps).

source.authors: Stanford JB, Willis SK, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Wise LA

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "selected apps FR (95% CI): 1.20 (1.13, 1.28); all other apps 1.21 (1.13, 1.30)" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: In time-varying analyses of cycle app use, fecundability increased 12-15% per cycle (selected apps FR 1.12, 95% CI 1.04-1.21; other apps FR 1.15, 95% CI 1.07-1.24).

source.title: Fecundability in relation to use of mobile computing apps to track the menstrual cycle
source.authors: Stanford JB, Willis SK, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Wise LA

facts.claim: Combined use of cycle apps with one or more fertility indicators (BBT, cervical fluid, cervix position, or urine LH) at baseline yielded higher fecundability than apps alone: selected apps with indicators FR 1.23 (1.14-1.34) vs. without 1.17 (1.05-1.30).

source.authors: Stanford JB, Willis SK, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Wise LA

facts.claim: Among women with a history of subfertility, use of selected cycle apps was associated with a 33% increase in fecundability (FR 1.33, 95% CI 1.05-1.67).

facts.claim: The mortality rate from cerebrovascular disease among oral contraceptive users has been estimated as 4.7 times that of non-users.

source.title: Strokes and contraceptive medication
source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The mortality rate from cerebrovascular disease in OC users has been estimated as 4.7 times that of non-users" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Most authorities advise against the use of oral contraceptives by heavy smokers due to evidence of synergistic potentiation of OC-associated cerebral thrombosis, especially for haemorrhagic strokes.

source.title: Strokes and contraceptive medication
source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "most authorities now advise against the use of OC by heavy smokers" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Reduction in oestrogen dosage in oral contraceptives has not been clearly demonstrated to reduce the risk of cardiac or cerebral disease.

source.title: Strokes and contraceptive medication
source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "It is also by no means clear that reduction in oestrogen dosage in oral contraceptives has reduced the risk of cardiac or cerebral disease" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: The risk of developing a thrombotic stroke among oral contraceptive users has been estimated as 9.5 times greater than among control groups.

source.title: Strokes and contraceptive medication
source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the risk of development of a thrombotic stroke 9.5 times greater than among control qroups" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: In the 15-34 years age group, the estimated excess annual death rate from oral contraceptive use is 1 per 20,000 ever-users.

source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "in the 15 to 34 years age group, the estimated excess annual death rate is 1 per 20000 ever-users" Section: Discussion.

source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "rising to 1 per 700 at the age from 45 to 49 vears" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: By age 35-44 years, oral contraceptive mortality considerably exceeds that of road traffic accident deaths in the United Kingdom.

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Cerebral infarction in young women has a relatively low mortality (9%)" Section: Discussion.

source.authors: Currie JN, Billings JJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A CT brain scan showed a small zone of infarction in the posterior limb of the left internal capsule." Section: Reports of Cases, Case 1.

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A CT brain scan demonstrated massive infarction of the territory of the left middle cerebral artery." Section: Reports of Cases, Case 2.

facts.claim: In a prospective Teen Bone Study (n=38, mean age 10.6), ovulation was documented no earlier than 10±5 months postmenarche; only 27 of 93 cycles (29%) were ovulatory while 66 (71%) were anovulatory.

source.authors: Seifert-Klauss V, Prior JC

facts.claim: Pooled meta-analysis of 5 prospective studies (n=458 premenopausal women) showed normally ovulatory cycles gained +0.53%/year spinal BMD versus -0.68%/year loss in women with ovulatory disturbances.

source.authors: Seifert-Klauss V, Prior JC

facts.claim: Pooled effectiveness estimate of emergency contraception from existing trials, after accounting for uncertainty in fertile-window prediction, was 50-60% with no heterogeneity between trials.

source.title: S28.3: Validity of methods used to estimate the effectiveness of Emergency Contraception
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "pooled estimate for the effectiveness of EC from existing trials accounting for uncertainty in prediction of the fertile window was 50-60%" Section: S28.3 abstract.

facts.claim: When intercourse was focused on the predicted fertile window, standard methods systematically overestimated emergency contraception effectiveness by 10-15% in absolute terms.

source.title: S28.3: Validity of methods used to estimate the effectiveness of Emergency Contraception
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "effectiveness was systematically overestimated by 10 to 15% (absolute)" Section: S28.3 results.

facts.claim: Overestimation of daily fecundity contributed an additional 10-20% overestimation of emergency contraception effectiveness in standard analyses.

source.title: S28.3: Validity of methods used to estimate the effectiveness of Emergency Contraception
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Due to overestimation of daily fecundity the effectiveness was overestimated by additional 10 to 20%" Section: S28.3 results.

facts.claim: Earlier analyses postulated emergency contraception effectiveness above 75%, but those estimates were too optimistic according to this validity simulation.

source.title: S28.3: Validity of methods used to estimate the effectiveness of Emergency Contraception
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Several earlier analyses postulated effectiveness above 75%" Section: S28.3 background.

facts.claim: Using only the preceding cycle length to predict index cycle length was equally sufficient to using the mean length of five previous cycles for EC effectiveness estimation.

source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.claim: After adjusting for fecund-window intercourse and couple clustering, three or more days of peri-implantation intercourse showed no effect on fecundability versus none (FR 1.00, 95% CrI 0.76-1.13).

source.authors: Hansen JL, Willis SK, Hu N, Thomas A, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "adjusted FR for three or more acts of peri-implantation intercourse versus none: 1.00, 95% credible interval: 0.76-1.13" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: Of 7,283 FERTILI cycles, 4,071 (55.9%) were excluded as not at risk for pregnancy due to no fecund-window intercourse, reflecting that many couples were avoiding pregnancy.

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability
source.authors: Hansen JL, Willis SK, Hu N, Thomas A, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "4071 cycles (55.9%) were not at risk for pregnancy because there was no intercourse during the known fecund window" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Approximately 3% or more of menstrual cycles in normal women may be anovulatory, which complicates fecund-window definitions based on cycle length alone.

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability
source.authors: Hansen JL, Willis SK, Hu N, Thomas A, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "this approach assumes that all cycles are ovulatory, while in reality 3% or more of cycles may be anovulatory" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: The FERTILI analysis included 2,606 cycles with fecund-window intercourse from 661 women, yielding 418 conception cycles.

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability

facts.claim: The peri-implantation window was defined as 5-9 days after estimated ovulation, and the fecund window as 5 days before through 3 days after estimated ovulation.

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability
source.authors: Hansen JL, Willis SK, Hu N, Thomas A, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The peri-implantation window was defined from 5 days to 9 days after estimated day of ovulation, inclusive." Section: Fecund and peri-implantation windows.

facts.claim: Sensitivity analysis using cervical mucus peak day as ovulation marker (1,583 cycles, 526 women) likewise found no effect of peri-implantation intercourse (FR 0.89, 95% CI 0.54-1.50 for 3+ days).

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability

facts.claim: Peak day of cervical mucus has accuracy similar to the LH surge for identifying ovulation.

source.title: Peri-implantation intercourse does not lower fecundability

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The peak day of cervical mucus has been shown to have similar accuracy to the LH surge to identify ovulation" Section: Fecund and peri-implantation windows.

facts.claim: Of 1,200 CNMs randomly selected from ACNM membership and surveyed, 514 responded (42.8% return rate); 450 (87.5% of respondents) were currently practicing CNMs usable for analysis.

source.authors: Fehring RJ, Hanson L, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Of the 514 respondents (42.8% return rate), 450 (87.5%) were currently practicing as CNMs." Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: CNMs projected on average that 17.1% of women using LAM would get pregnant unexpectedly within 6 months (range 0-65%, SD 14.12), substantially overestimating LAM failure compared to expert consensus of <2%.

source.authors: Fehring RJ, Hanson L, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Serum α-carotene levels (per log unit (μg/dL) increase, fecundability odds ratio (FOR) = 1.17, 95% confidence interval (CI): 1.00, 1.36)" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: 92.4% of EAGeR Trial participants reported having taken vitamin supplements, a factor that may increase plasma micronutrient levels and affect bioavailability of other micronutrients.

source.title: Shorter Time to Pregnancy With Increasing Preconception Carotene Concentrations  Among Women With 1-2 Previous Pregnancy Losses

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Most women reported having taken vitamin supplements (92.4%)." Section: Results.

facts.claim: Preconception serum α-carotene and γ-tocopherol concentrations were inversely correlated (r = -0.08), and β-carotene with γ-tocopherol showed r = -0.20, suggesting competitive absorption between carotenes and tocopherols.

facts.claim: Cryptoxanthin concentrations were higher in nulliparous women (8.1 μg/dL) than in women with 1 prior live birth (7.4 μg/dL) or 2 prior live births (7.5 μg/dL) (P < 0.01).

source.title: Shorter Time to Pregnancy With Increasing Preconception Carotene Concentrations  Among Women With 1-2 Previous Pregnancy Losses

facts.claim: Serum α-carotene concentrations at or above the US average (≥2.92 μg/dL) were associated with shorter time to pregnancy compared to below-average levels (FOR = 1.21, 95% CI: 1.02, 1.44).

source.title: Shorter Time to Pregnancy With Increasing Preconception Carotene Concentrations  Among Women With 1-2 Previous Pregnancy Losses

facts.claim: Preconception serum γ-tocopherol concentrations at or above the US average (≥187 μg/dL) were associated with longer time to pregnancy compared to below-average levels (adjusted FOR = 0.83, 95% CI: 0.69, 1.00).

source.title: Shorter Time to Pregnancy With Increasing Preconception Carotene Concentrations  Among Women With 1-2 Previous Pregnancy Losses

facts.claim: In unadjusted analysis, each log-unit increase in serum γ-tocopherol was associated with substantially longer time to pregnancy (FOR = 0.66, 95% CI: 0.52, 0.84) among women with 1-2 prior pregnancy losses.

facts.claim: In the EAGeR Trial cohort, 22.0%, 16.2%, 10.8%, 7.3%, 5.1%, and 3.8% of women achieved pregnancy during menstrual cycles 1-6 respectively; 34.8% did not achieve pregnancy within 6 cycles or withdrew.

facts.claim: The CEIBA study was a prospective cohort study conducted at 17 Creighton Model FertilityCare Centers (16 in the USA and one in Toronto, Canada) to assess fertility motivations, intentions, behaviors, and effectiveness among new CrM users.

source.title: Enrollment, Childbearing Motivations, and Intentions of Couples in the Creighton Model Effectiveness, Intentions, and Behaviors Assessment (CEIBA) Study
source.authors: Porucznik CA, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "We conducted this prospective cohort study at 17 CrM FertilityCareTM Centers; 16 in the USA and one in Toronto, Canada." Section: Settings and Design.

facts.claim: CEIBA couples had higher mean positive childbearing motivation (3.3 vs 2.9/2.8) and higher median desired total children (4 vs 2.5) compared with a 1995 California cohort of 401 married couples.

source.title: Enrollment, Childbearing Motivations, and Intentions of Couples in the Creighton Model Effectiveness, Intentions, and Behaviors Assessment (CEIBA) Study
source.authors: Porucznik CA, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "our sample had higher positive childbearing motivation scores (mean 3.3 for both women and men)" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: CEIBA exclusion criteria for assessing pregnancy-avoidance effectiveness include women under 18 or over 40, prior CrM use within past 6 months, current pregnancy, current hormonal contraception, and fewer than two menses in past 3 months.

source.title: Enrollment, Childbearing Motivations, and Intentions of Couples in the Creighton Model Effectiveness, Intentions, and Behaviors Assessment (CEIBA) Study
source.authors: Porucznik CA, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Have previously used the CrMS within past 6 months for family planning 20 (2.8) ... Fewer than two menses in the past 3 months" Section: Table 1.

facts.claim: The CrM teaches couples radical autonomy regarding intercourse decisions on any given day with full knowledge of pregnancy possibility, with no prescribed requirement regarding pregnancy outcomes.

source.title: Enrollment, Childbearing Motivations, and Intentions of Couples in the Creighton Model Effectiveness, Intentions, and Behaviors Assessment (CEIBA) Study

facts.claim: At enrollment, 21% of CEIBA women intended to have a child within a year and 60% between 1 and 3 years, despite all couples beginning Creighton Model use to avoid pregnancy.

facts.claim: In the ENDO Study operative cohort, the incidence of surgically visualized endometriosis was 40% (190/473), with 11.8% moderate/severe and 28.3% minimal/mild disease.

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
source.authors: Peterson CM, Johnstone EB, Hammoud AO, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Kennedy A, Chen Z, Sun L, Fujimoto VY, Hediger ML, Buck Louis GM, ENDO Study Working Group

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "incidence of surgically visualized endometriosis in the operative cohort was 40% (190/473 with 11.8% [56/473] moderate/severe" Section: Results.

facts.claim: The reported prevalence of endometriosis varies widely by study population: 0.7-11% in healthcare-seeking women, 2-22% in surgical sterilization patients, 17-47% in infertile women, and 2-74% in women with chronic pelvic pain.

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
source.authors: Peterson CM, Johnstone EB, Hammoud AO, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Kennedy A, Chen Z, Sun L, Fujimoto VY, Hediger ML, Buck Louis GM, ENDO Study Working Group

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "0.7–11% in populations presenting for health care, 2–22% when undergoing surgical sterilization, 17–47% among infertile women, and 2–74%" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: When restricting to stage 3-4 endometriosis (11.8%, 56/473 of operative cohort), parity remained strongly protective (AOR 0.19; 95% CI 0.10-0.37) and age at first sex predictive (AOR 1.11; 95% CI 1.03-1.19).

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "parity (AOR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.10–0.37), age at first sex (AOR, 1.11; 95% CI, 1.03–1.19)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: More than 80% of women in all study groups had a history of oral contraceptive use, which may have masked menstrual cycle differences between women with and without endometriosis.

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: MRI cannot reliably diagnose minimal/mild (stage 1-2) endometriosis, limiting population-cohort studies that rely on noninvasive imaging for case identification.

source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: In the ENDO Study population cohort, MRI-visualized endometriosis incidence was 11% (14/127), consisting primarily of ovarian endometriomas and nodular implants (rASRM stage 3-4).

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: A history of infertility increased the odds of endometriosis diagnosis more than 2-fold in the operative cohort (AOR 2.43; 95% CI 1.57-3.76) after adjusting for age and study site.

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: A history of infertility increased the odds of endometriosis diagnosis more than 7-fold in the population cohort (AOR 7.91; 95% CI 1.69-37.2), the only consistent risk factor across both cohorts.

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: Women with endometriosis in the operative cohort had lower mean gravidity (1.7 ± 2.0) than unaffected women (2.3 ± 2.1), and lower parity (1.8 ± 1.3 vs 2.2 ± 1.4).

source.title: Risk factors associated with endometriosis: importance of study population for characterizing disease in the ENDO Study
source.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology

facts.claim: In the operative cohort, increased BMI was associated with decreased odds of endometriosis (AOR 0.95; 95% CI 0.93-0.98), while gravidity (AOR 0.49) and parity (AOR 0.42) were also protective.

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "academic expert surgeons had substantial interrater reliability (Fleiss's κ=0.79, 95% CI 0.70–0.88)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Interrater reliability for revised ASRM endometriosis staging was only moderate, with Fleiss kappa 0.44 (95% CI 0.41-0.47) for experienced clinical assessment.

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "moderate interrater reliability when based on experienced assessment (Fleiss κ=0.44, 95% CI 0.41–0.47" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Intrarater agreement between expert clinical staging and computer-assisted revised ASRM staging was almost perfect, with approximately 90% identical classification (mean weighted kappa 0.95, range 0.89-0.99).

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "approximately 90% of women identically classified (range: 83%–97%) and almost perfect agreement (mean weighted κ=0.95, range: 0.89–0.99)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Academic expert surgeons rated lower endometriosis severity than local experts: 58% stage I, 24% II, 10% III, 8% IV vs. 47% I, 28% II, 16% III, 10% IV among local specialized experts.

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "academic expert surgeons rated a lower incidence of stage II – IV (58% as stage I, 24% as stage II, 10% as stage III, and 8% as stage IV)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Endometriosis was reported in approximately 66% of the 148 ENDO Study women with images, with little variation between rater types (65% academic vs 66% local specialized).

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Endometriosis diagnosis was reported for approximately 66% of women, with little variation by type of rater" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Bland-Altman analysis showed limits of agreement between any two raters ranged from 38 points below to 49 points above (average 32 below to 33 above) for revised ASRM total endometriosis scores.

source.title: Interrater and intrarater reliability in the diagnosis and staging of endometriosis
source.authors: Schliep KC, Stanford JB, Chen Z, Zhang B, Dorais JK, Boiman Johnstone E, Hammoud AO, Varner MW, Louis GM, Peterson CM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Limits of agreement between any two raters ranged from 38 points below to 49 points above, with an average of 32 points below to 33 points above." Section: Results.

facts.claim: With ovulation disruption alone, modeled effectiveness of levonorgestrel EC ranged from 49% (no delay) to 8% (72-hour delay between intercourse and EC administration).

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "With disruption of ovulation alone, the potential effectiveness of levonorgestrel EC ranged from 49% (no delay) to 8% (72-hour delay)." Section: Abstract Result(s).

facts.claim: With a 72-hour or greater delay, postfertilization mechanisms must be operative for LNG EC to obtain effectiveness of 50% or more, even assuming complete prefertilization inhibition.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a delay of 72 hours or more would require postfertilization mechanisms to be operative to obtain an effectiveness of 50% or more." Section: Results.

facts.claim: In vitro studies at LNG concentrations used for EC have not demonstrated effects on human spermatozoa function, acrosome reaction, or zona-binding capacity.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Effects on human spermatozoa were not demonstrated in vitro for LNG concentrations as used in EC" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: The discrepancy between LNG EC clinical-trial effectiveness and modeled ovulation-disruption effectiveness implies either inflated trial estimates or postfertilization mechanisms of action contribute to pregnancy reduction.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "mechanisms other than disturbance of ovulation contribute to the reduction of clinical pregnancy, including mechanisms acting after fertilization." Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: At 96 hours or more after intercourse, the maximum possible ability of levonorgestrel EC to intervene before fertilization is essentially zero, because most women have already ovulated.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "if LNG EC is taken 96 hours or more after intercourse, the maximum possible ability of LNG EC to intervene before fertilization is essentially zero." Section: Results.

facts.claim: In cycles with follicular diameter ≥18 mm at LNG-EC administration, only 2 of 17 LNG cycles avoided rupture within 5 days versus 2 of 16 placebo cycles, indicating minimal ovulation suppression near ovulation.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Reduction factor for fecundity a 0.143 0.167 0.605" Section: Table 1 (12-14, 15-17, ≥18 mm follicle strata).

facts.claim: Mean follicular diameter before rupture in the modeling cohort was 21.7 mm (SD 2.8) with a relatively constant growth rate of approximately 1.5 mm per day.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the rate of follicular growth is reasonably constant across cycles (1.5 mm per day)... mean [SD] follicular size before rupture of 21.7 ± 2.8 mm" Section: Results.

facts.claim: The follicular growth equation derived from 533 ultrasound measurements in 327 cycles from 107 women: follicular diameter (mm) = 21.6747 − 1.5032 × (days to ovulation) + ε, where ε ~ N(0, 2.755).

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Follicular diameter (mm) = 21.6747 − 1.5032 × (Days to ovulation) + e" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Recent analyses suggest the overall effectiveness of LNG EC has been overestimated by approximately 10% (absolute difference) by the standard estimation procedures used in clinical trials.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the overall effectiveness has indeed been overestimated by approximately 10% (absolute difference)" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: With a 24-hour or greater delay between intercourse and LNG-EC administration, mechanisms beyond ovulation disruption are required to obtain a clinical effectiveness of 50% or more.

source.title: Levonorgestrel emergency contraception: a joint analysis of effectiveness and mechanism of action
source.authors: Mikolajczyk RT, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "With a delay of 24 hours or more, other mechanisms of action are required to obtain an effectiveness of 50% or more." Section: Results.

facts.claim: In the ENDO Study operative cohort, the incidence of surgically visualized endometriosis was 41%.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The incidence of surgically visualized endometriosis for the operative cohort was 41%" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Among asymptomatic fertile women undergoing tubal sterilization procedures, endometriosis incidence ranges from 4% to 43%.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Among asymptomatic fertile women undergoing tubal sterilization procedures, incidence ranges from 4% – 43%" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In the ENDO operative cohort, leading surgical indications were pelvic pain (42%), pelvic mass (15%), and menstrual irregularities (12%).

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Leading reasons for surgery were pelvic pain (42%), pelvic mass (15%) and menstrual irregularities (12%)." Section: Results.

facts.claim: Approximately 5% of women in each ENDO cohort screened positive for severe depression or suicidal ideation requiring clinical follow-up, comparable to the 8% U.S. adult rate.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "smaller but still notable percentage (≈5%) of women in each cohort who screened positive for severe depression or suicidal ideation" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: The ENDO Study used the Revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine (rASRM) staging: stage I (1-5), stage II (6-15), stage III (16-40), and stage IV (>40).

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "stage I (scores 1–5), stage II (scores 6–15), stage III (scores 16–40), and stage IV (scores > 40)" Section: Operational Definitions.

facts.claim: In the ENDO Study population cohort of menstruating women not seeking surgical care, the incidence of MRI-visualized endometriosis was 11%.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "In the population cohort, the overall incidence of MRI visualized endometriosis was 11%." Section: Results.

facts.claim: Among ENDO operative cohort women undergoing all three diagnostic methods, 34% had endometriosis diagnosed concurrently by visualization, histology, and MRI.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "34% had endometriosis diagnosed by visualization, histology and MRI" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Endometriosis incidence in the ENDO operative cohort varied by diagnostic method: 0.7% for histology only, 7% for MRI only, and 41% for visualized disease.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "varied by an order of two depending upon the diagnostic method (0.7% for histology only, 75 for MRI only and 41% for visualized disease)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Among women with endometriosis in the ENDO operative cohort, the majority had minimal/mild (stage 1) disease, with 73% staged as minimal or mild.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "only 41% of women had visualized disease with staging skewed to minimal or mild (73%) disease" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Concordance between surgeon's categorical rASRM staging and the algorithm-computed staging was 98% for stage 0, 70% for stage 1, 37% for stage 2, 61% for stage 3, and 43% for stage 4.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "98% for stage 0 or no endometriosis; 70% for stage 1; 37% for stage 2; 61% for stage 3; and 43% for stage 4" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Staging concordance in the ENDO Study improved to 84% when dichotomized as minimal/mild versus moderate/severe disease.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "concordance increased (84%) when dichotomizing staging as minimal/mild verse moderate/severe" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Stratton et al reported MRI sensitivity of 69% and specificity of 75% for endometriosis detection relative to histologically confirmed disease.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Stratton and colleagues (29) reported MRI sensitivity and specificity relative to histologically confirmed disease to be 69% and 75%, respectively" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Among women undergoing pelvic surgery for non-endometriosis indications, 30%-50% are diagnosed with incidental endometriosis on visualization.

source.title: Incidence of endometriosis by study population and diagnostic method: the ENDO  study
source.authors: Buck Louis GM, Hediger ML, Peterson CM, Croughan M, Sundaram R, Stanford J, Chen Z, Fujimoto VY, Varner MW, Trumble A, Giudice LC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "30% – 50% of women undergoing surgical procedures that fortuitously allow visualization are diagnosed with incidental endometriosis" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: Cross-sectional analysis of 311,064 prospectively measured menstrual cycles from 35,940 European and North American women aged 18-40 collected 2019-2021.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "35,940 European and North American women aged 18–40 years" and "prospectively measured menstrual cycles (311,064 cycles) over 3 full years (2019–2021)" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: The lunar synodic month averages 29.5 days, divided in this study into 8 standard phases of 3-4 days each using the lunar package of R software.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "averaging 29.5 days in length" and "The lunar cycle was divided into 8 standard phases of 3–4 days each" Section: Abstract and Variables.

facts.claim: Menstrual onset occurred less frequently during Waxing crescent and First quarter phases than during Waxing gibbous, Waning gibbous, or Last quarter phases.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "first day of menstruation occurred less frequently in the Waxing crescent and First quarter than in the Waxing, Waning gibbous, or Last quarter" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Premenstrual spotting incidence is approximately 10% and was acknowledged as a potential limitation that could shift perceived menstrual onset.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the incidence of premenstrual spotting may be approximately 10%" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Higher progesterone levels after ovulation raise basal body temperature by approximately 0.5 degrees Fahrenheit.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Higher progesterone levels after ovulation make the body temperature rise by approximately 0.5F" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Of 35,940 women, 15,342 (42.69%) lived in Europe and 20,598 (57.31%) lived in North America.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "15,342 (42.69%) and 20,598 (57.31%) lived in Europe and North America, respectively" Section: Results.

facts.claim: A statistically significant circaseptan (7-day) rhythm of menstrual onset was observed with peak occurrence (acrophase) on Thursdays and Fridays.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a circaseptan (7-day) rhythmicity of menstruation was observed, with a peak (acrophase) of menstrual onset on Thursdays and Fridays" Section: Abstract Results.

facts.claim: The circaseptan rhythm of menstruation was most pronounced for cycle durations between 27 and 29 days compared to shorter or longer cycles.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "This feature was most pronounced for cycle durations between 27 and 29 days" Section: Abstract Results.

facts.claim: In winter, the circaseptan rhythm appeared in cycles of 27-29 days but was absent in cycles of other lengths.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "In winter, the circaseptan rhythm was found in cycles of 27–29 days, but not in other cycle lengths" Section: Abstract Results.

facts.claim: A circalunar rhythm of menstrual onset was also statistically significant but less pronounced than the circaseptan rhythm, with Rho values approximately 3 times lower.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Rho values in Supplemental Table 3 that are approximately 3 times lower than those observed for the circaseptan rhythm" Section: Results.

facts.claim: The chi-squared test for variation of menstrual onset across days of the week was highly significant (P < 2.2E-16).

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a clear and highly significant (P < 2.2E-16) variation of the frequency of menstrual onset across the days of the week" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Variation in menstrual onset across lunar phases was statistically significant at P < 1E-11, though less pronounced than circaseptan variation.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Although the variation was less than for the circaseptan cycle, it was statistically significant (P <1E-11)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Inclusion required at least 12 days of logged basal body temperature (BBT) per cycle with no more than 30 days between two logged BBT points.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "at least 12 days with logged basal body temperature (BBT) points during the cycle and there was a maximum of 30 days between 2 logged BBT points" Section: Material and Methods.

facts.claim: Exclusion criteria included known polycystic ovarian syndrome, thyroid disease, endometriosis, and menopause.

source.title: The menstrual cycle is influenced by weekly and lunar rhythms
source.authors: Ecochard R, Leiva R, Bouchard TP, Van Lamsweerde A, Pearson JT, Stanford JB, Gronfier C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Exclusion criteria included known polycystic-ovarian syndrome, thyroid disease, endometriosis, and menopause" Section: Women and Cycles.

facts.claim: The EAGeR trial was a multi-center, double-blind, block-randomized, placebo-controlled trial evaluating low-dose aspirin effect on live birth in 1228 women with a history of one to two previous pregnancy losses.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "placebo-controlled trial evaluating the effect of low dose aspirin (LDA) on live birth in 1228 women with a history of one to two previous losses" Section: Study Design and Population.

facts.claim: Pregnancy loss affects up to 15% of clinically confirmed pregnancies and an estimated 30% of all conceptions when very early losses are included.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Pregnancy loss is common, affecting up to 15% of all clinically confirmed pregnancies" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In the EAGeR cohort, mean maternal age was highest in the low AMH group and lowest in the high AMH group (p<0.001), consistent with AMH decline with age.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Mean age was highest in the low AMH group and lowest in the high AMH group (p<0.001)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: hCG-detected pregnancy was defined as a positive urine test sensitive to 25 mIU/mL hCG; daily first-morning urine collection in the first two cycles enabled detection of very early pregnancies and losses.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a positive result on a urine pregnancy test sensitive to hCG level of 25 mIU/ml" Section: hCG detected pregnancy and detection of hCG loss.

facts.claim: AMH likely does not have clinical utility for preconception counseling about future pregnancy loss risk in women with a history of one or two prior pregnancy losses.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "AMH likely does not purport clinical utility for preconception counseling of future pregnancy loss risk" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: EAGeR participants used the Clearblue Easy Fertility Monitor to assist with timing intercourse and scheduling clinic visits.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Fertility monitors were used to assist with timing intercourse and scheduling clinic visits (Clearblue Easy Fertility Monitor; Inverness Medical)" Section: Study Design and Population.

facts.claim: AMH categorical thresholds used in the analysis were low (<1.00 ng/mL), normal (1.00-3.5 ng/mL), and high (>3.5 ng/mL), based on clinical thresholds derived from infertile women.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "low (<1.00 ng/mL), normal (1.00 to 3.5 ng/mL), and high (>3.5 ng/mL)" Section: Statistical analysis.

facts.claim: Among 708 women who achieved a clinical pregnancy, 10.3% with low AMH, 10.3% with normal AMH (61), and 10.4% with high AMH (50) experienced a clinical loss; clinical loss in low AMH group was 15.3% (19/124).

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "19 (15.3%) women with low AMH experiencing a clinical loss, compared to 61 (10.3%) with normal AMH and 50 (10.4%) with a high AMH level" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Of 55 pregnancy losses with determinate cytogenetic results, 26 were euploid and 29 were aneuploid, with autosomal trisomy the predominant aneuploidy (n=15).

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "normal (n=26, euploid), or abnormal (n=29, aneuploid) with the majority of aneuploid cases diagnosed as an autosomal trisomy (n=15)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Preconception AMH levels showed no significant association with aneuploidy of clinically confirmed pregnancy losses in unadjusted or adjusted models.

source.title: Antimüllerian hormone and pregnancy loss from the Effects of Aspirin in Gestation and Reproduction trial
source.authors: Zarek SM, Mitchell EM, Sjaarda LA, Mumford SL, Silver RM, Stanford JB, Galai N, Schliep KC, Radin RG, Plowden TC, DeCherney AH, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "no significant associations of low or high AMH with an increased risk of aneuploidy of clinically confirmed pregnancy losses" Section: Results.

facts.claim: In a population-based HUNT3 cohort study of over 3,000 Norwegian women, the point prevalence of ovulatory disturbances in normal-length menstrual cycles was 24%–37%.

source.title: Prospective analyses of sex/gender-related publication decisions in general  medical journals: editorial rejection of population-based women's reproductive  physiology
source.authors: Kalidasan D, Goshtasebi A, Chrisler J, Brown HL, Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the point prevalence of ovulatory disturbances was 24%–37% in normal-length cycles in over 3000 women" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In singleton births, ART was associated with preterm birth (<37 weeks) compared with women who never used fertility treatment, with OR 3.28 (95% CI 1.74-6.20).

source.title: Fertility treatments and adverse perinatal outcomes in a population-based sampling of births in Florida, Maryland, and Utah: a cross-sectional study
source.journal: BJOG : an International Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "clear, slippery, lubricative mucus, having the physical characteristics of raw white of egg (Spinnbarkheit); this has been termed the "peak symptom"" Section: Symptoms of Ovulation.

facts.claim: The onset of mucus symptoms occurred a mean of 6.2 days before ovulation, providing advance warning of impending ovulation.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The onset of mucus symptoms occurred 6·2 days (mean) before ovulation." Section: Summary.

facts.claim: Ovulation, as assessed hormonally, occurred a mean of 0.9 days after the peak symptom, ranging from 3 days after to 2 days before in one subject.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "ovulation, as assessed hormonally, occurred 0-9 days (mean) after the occurrence of the peak symptom, with a range from 3 days after to 2 days before" Section: Results.

facts.claim: The mean interval from onset of mucus symptom to ovulation was 6.2 days, with a range of 3 to 10 days across the 22 subjects.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the mean interval from the onset of mucus symptom to ovulation was 6-2 days, with a range from 3 to 10 days." Section: Results.

facts.claim: The basal body temperature rise generally occurred 1-2 days after ovulation, making BBT useful only retrospectively for identifying ovulation.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The rise in B.B.T. generally occurred 1-2 days after ovulation." Section: Results.

facts.claim: The day of ovulation was operationally defined as the day after the midcycle plasma luteinising hormone peak, corresponding to urinary pregnanediol exceeding twice the mean follicular-phase level.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "We defined the day of ovulation as the day after the midcycle plasma-L.H. peak" Section: Hormonal Estimate of Day of Ovulation.

facts.claim: The midcycle peak of plasma LH occurs about 16 hours before ovulation, as judged by corpus luteum biopsy.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The midcycle peak of plasma-L.H. occurs about 16 hours before ovulation, as judged by biopsy of the corpus luteum" Section: Hormonal Estimate of Day of Ovulation.

facts.claim: With one exception, the peak of urinary oestrogen excretion occurred 0-2 days before the estimated day of ovulation in study subjects.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the peak of urinary oestrogen excretion occurred 0-2 days before the estimated day of ovulation." Section: Hormonal Estimate of Day of Ovulation.

facts.claim: The peak symptom (lubricative clear mucus) characteristically lasts 1-2 days, with the last day of occurrence taken as the day of the peak symptom.

source.title: Symptoms and hormonal changes accompanying ovulation
source.authors: Billings EL, Brown JB, Billings JJ, Burger HG

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "It characteristically lasts for 1-2 days, and the last day of its occurrence is referred to as the day of the peak symptom" Section: Symptoms of Ovulation.

facts.claim: In the PROMISE trial, nearly 33% of women screened (515 of 1568) were excluded because of subfertility, meaning the trial did not address progesterone supplementation in women with coexisting subfertility.

source.title: Progesterone in Women with Recurrent Miscarriages
source.journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
source.authors: Carpentier PA, Stanford JB, Boyle PC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Nearly 33% of the women screened for the trial were excluded because of subfertility (515 of 1568 women)." Section: Letter to the Editor.

facts.claim: In the PROMISE trial, progesterone administration began after implantation rather than before or at the time of implantation, potentially missing progesterone's key role in embryo implantation.

source.title: Progesterone in Women with Recurrent Miscarriages
source.journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
source.authors: Carpentier PA, Stanford JB, Boyle PC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "in the trial by Coomarasamy et al., administration began after implantation." Section: Letter to the Editor.

facts.claim: A short luteal phase of less than 10 days is associated with a lower probability of clinical pregnancy and may also be associated with miscarriage.

source.title: Progesterone in Women with Recurrent Miscarriages
source.journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
source.authors: Carpentier PA, Stanford JB, Boyle PC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a short luteal phase (<10 days) is associated with a lower probability of clinical pregnancy, and it may also be associated with miscarriage." Section: Letter to the Editor.

facts.claim: The PROMISE trial authors acknowledge there are no accepted histologic, biochemical, proteomic, genomic, or systems-biology techniques to identify luteal-phase defect, and that a short cycle is only a crude marker.

source.title: Progesterone in Women with Recurrent Miscarriages

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "there are no accepted histologic, biochemical, proteomic, genomic, or other systems-biology-based techniques to identify luteal-phase defect" Section: Authors Reply.

facts.claim: Systematic review of 8,755 unique citations identified 53 studies meeting inclusion criteria for prospective fertility awareness-based method effectiveness research.

facts.claim: Early pregnancy factor (EPF) can be detected in maternal serum as early as 1 to 2 days after fertilization and remains detectable through most of pregnancy as long as the embryo is viable.

source.journal: American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
source.authors: Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "it can be detected as early as 1 to 2 days after fertilization and continuing through most of the pregnancy as long as it remains viable" Section: Biochemical evidence for fertilization.

facts.claim: Recent studies suggest a maximum possible per-cycle clinical pregnancy rate of 0.33 with completely optimized timing of intercourse, with median time to pregnancy of 3 months.

source.title: Mechanisms of action of intrauterine devices: update and estimation of postfertilization effects
source.journal: American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
source.authors: Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "consistent with recent studies that suggest a maximum possible per cycle pregnancy rate of 0.33 with completely optimized intercourse" Section: Estimating the contribution of postfertilization effects.

facts.claim: Direct observation of recovered ova showed fertilization in 14/18 (77%) control cycles versus 1/4 (25%) inert IUD cycles, 0/5 copper IUD cycles, and 1/5 (20%) hormonal IUD cycles.

source.authors: Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Ortiz et al (1996) ... 14/18 0.77 (0.52-0.94)" Section: Table II Evidence of fertilization.

facts.claim: Patients considering use of the IUD should be informed about its mechanisms of action, including postfertilization effects, particularly for those who may object to forms of birth control acting after fertilization.

facts.claim: Up to 85% of women using the levonorgestrel IUD remain ovulatory during its use, indicating it does not primarily act by suppressing ovulation.

facts.claim: The Intermountain Fallout Cohort enrolled 4,818 schoolchildren in grades 6-12 in 1965 from Washington County Utah, Graham County Arizona, and Lincoln County Nevada with varying radioiodine exposure from above-ground nuclear weapons tests at the Nevada Test Site.

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "identified a cohort of 4,818 schoolchildren in grades 6–12 who had varying exposure to radioiodine" Section: Materials and Methods / The Intermountain Fallout Cohort.

facts.claim: In the phase III analysis subcohort of 1,466 participants, 274 (18.7%) were classified as infertile and 30 were classified as sterile, using a definition of infertility as having tried to conceive for one year or longer before age 40.

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "1,466 participants who were classified as fertile (n = 1,115 fertile) or infertile (n = 274)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Compared with the lowest dose tertile, the adjusted odds ratio for infertility in the highest tertile was 1.35 (95% CI 0.96-1.90); when thyroid disease was added to the model the AOR attenuated to 1.29 (95% CI 0.91-1.83).

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.claim: In the quartile analysis, the third quartile of radioiodine dose was significantly associated with infertility versus the lowest quartile (unadjusted OR 1.64, 95% CI 1.13-2.38; AOR 1.62, 95% CI 1.09-2.41).

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "unadjusted OR = 1.64 (95% CI: 1.13, 2.38); AOR = 1.62 (95% CI: 1.09, 2.41)" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Medically confirmed thyroid disease was an independent risk factor for infertility in adjusted models including radioiodine dose: AOR = 1.78 (95% CI 1.13-2.82); a similar estimate was obtained without dose in the model (AOR = 1.84, 95% CI 1.16-2.92).

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "thyroid disease itself was a significant risk factor for infertility: AOR = 1.78 (95% CI: 1.13, 2.82)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: No association was observed between radioiodine dose and sterility: AOR 1.02 (95% CI 0.35-2.94) for the middle tertile and 1.11 (95% CI 0.42-2.87) for the upper tertile versus the lowest tertile.

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "AOR = 1.02; 95% CI: 0.35, 2.94 and AOR = 1.11; 95% CI: 0.42, 2.87" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Infertility was operationally defined as having tried to become pregnant (or to father a pregnancy) for one year or longer before age 40, not precluding pregnancies before or after the infertility episode.

source.title: Childhood thyroid radioiodine exposure and subsequent infertility in the intermountain fallout cohort
source.authors: Stone MB, Stanford JB, Lyon JL, VanDerslice JA, Alder SC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "considered infertility to exist if the respondent had answered yes, and that this happened at < 40 years of age" Section: Materials and Methods / Infertility.

facts.claim: In 2488 ovulatory cycles from 528 women without known subfertility, the mean number of days of peak type (estrogenic) cervical mucus per cycle was 6.4 (SD 4.2; median 6; IQR 4-8).

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The mean (SD) days of estrogenic (peak type) mucus per cycle (a conservative indicator of the fertile window) was 6.4 (4.2) days" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: The mean number of any potentially fertile days per cycle (a broader clinical indicator of the fertile window) was 12.1 days (SD 5.4; median 11; IQR 9-14) in women without known subfertility.

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The mean (SD) number of any potentially fertile days (a broader clinical indicator of the fertile window) was 12.1 (5.4) days" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: Nulliparous women age 30+ had significantly fewer mean days of peak type mucus than nulliparous women age <30 (5.3 vs 6.4 days, P=0.02), and fewer potentially fertile days (11.8 vs 13.9 days, P<0.01).

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "fewer mean days of peak type mucus per cycle (5.3 versus 6.4 days, P=0.02), and fewer potentially fertile days (11.8 versus 13.9 days, P<0.01)" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: Compared to nulliparous women age <30, nulliparous women age 30+ had a 1.90 (95% CI 1.18-3.06) higher prevalence ratio for cycles with <=2 days of peak type mucus.

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "prevalence ratio of cycles with peak type mucus 2 days... 1.90 (95% confidence interval (CI) 1.18, 3.06)" Section: Main Results.

facts.claim: Mean cycle length in this pooled cohort was 30.6 days (95% CI 30.1-31.1), with mean follicular phase 18.8 days (95% CI 18.3-19.3) and mean luteal phase 11.7 days (95% CI 11.5-11.9).

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The mean cycle length was 30.6 days... mean follicular phase was longer than the mean luteal phase, 18.8 days... versus 11.7 days" Section: Ovulation and cycle phase lengths.

facts.claim: Only 8.6% of ovulatory cycles had an estimated day of ovulation (mucus peak day) on cycle day 14, and 11.0% on day 15, contradicting the assumption that ovulation typically occurs on day 14.

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Only 8.6% of cycles had an estimated day of ovulation (peak day) on day 14; and 11.0% on day 15." Section: Ovulation and cycle phase lengths.

facts.claim: The mean cervical mucus cycle score (Hilgers score) across all cycles was 8.2 (95% CI 7.9-8.4) on a 0-16 scale where 16 indicates maximum fecundity.

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The mean cervical mucus cycle score was 8.2 (95% CI 7.9, 8.4)." Section: Cervical mucus type and quality score.

facts.claim: Women age 35+ had a lower cervical mucus cycle score than women <35 (7.3, 95% CI 6.0-8.6 vs 8.7, 95% CI 8.0-9.4), reflecting age-related decline in mucus quality.

source.title: Cervical mucus patterns and the fertile window in women without known subfertility: a pooled analysis of three cohorts
source.authors: Najmabadi S, Schliep KC, Simonsen SE, Porucznik CA, Egger MJ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women aged 35 years compared to women <35 years had lower cervical mucus cycle score of 7.3 (95% CI 6.0, 8.6) versus 8.7 (95% CI 8.0, 9.4)" Section: Further analyses by age and parity.

facts.claim: At least 10% of couples in the United States have had difficulty achieving pregnancy.

source.title: Methodologic and statistical approaches to studying human fertility and environmental exposure
source.authors: Tingen C, Stanford JB, Dunson DB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "At least 10% of couples in the United States have had difficulty achieving pregnancy" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: The European Study of Daily Fecundability (Colombo and Masarotto 2000) collected data from 881 couples across 7,017 menstrual cycles.

source.title: Methodologic and statistical approaches to studying human fertility and environmental exposure
source.authors: Tingen C, Stanford JB, Dunson DB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the European Study of Daily Fecundability (Colombo and Masarotto 2000), with 881 couples and 7,017 menstrual cycles" Section: Methods of analysis.

facts.claim: Delays in time to pregnancy increase the risk of adverse perinatal outcomes such as low birth weight or preterm delivery.

source.title: Methodologic and statistical approaches to studying human fertility and environmental exposure
source.authors: Tingen C, Stanford JB, Dunson DB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "delays in TTP are reported to increase the risk of adverse perinatal outcomes such as low birth weight or preterm delivery" Section: Integrating the study.

facts.claim: For studies of common exposures and overall fecundability, more than 100 couples followed until conception or at least 6-12 months is needed.

source.title: Methodologic and statistical approaches to studying human fertility and environmental exposure
source.authors: Tingen C, Stanford JB, Dunson DB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "an excess of 100 couples followed until conception or at least 6–12 months if not conceiving is needed" Section: Methods of analysis.

facts.claim: Detailed prospective studies allowing inferences on day-specific probabilities of conception should be considered the gold standard for studying environmental exposure effects on fertility.

source.title: Methodologic and statistical approaches to studying human fertility and environmental exposure

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the postovulatory (luteal) phase is relatively constant at approximately 14 days" Section: Detailed Prospective Studies.

source.authors: Stanford JB

source.authors: Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the egg is viable for 12 hours or less" Section: Letter to the Editor (Stanford).

facts.claim: Among eumenorrheic women without known subfertility, the reported per-cycle incidence of sporadic anovulation ranges from 1.0% to 14.5%, defined by LH surge or luteal progesterone.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study
source.authors: DeVilbiss EA, Stanford JB, Mumford SL, Sjaarda LA, Kim K, Zolton JR, Perkins NJ, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the reported per-cycle incidence of sporadic anovulation is highly variable, ranging from 1.0 to 14.5%" Section: Background.

facts.claim: In presumed fertile women with regular cycles, routine testing to ensure ovulation and treatment of sporadic anovulation are unlikely to be medically necessary.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study
source.authors: DeVilbiss EA, Stanford JB, Mumford SL, Sjaarda LA, Kim K, Zolton JR, Perkins NJ, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "routine testing to ensure ovulation and treatment of anovulation are unlikely to be medically necessary" Section: Conclusions/Abstract.

facts.claim: Transvaginal sonographic detection found only one anovulatory cycle among 150 cycles in 53 women, suggesting true anovulation incidence is on the lower end of the 1.0-14.5% range.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study
source.authors: DeVilbiss EA, Stanford JB, Mumford SL, Sjaarda LA, Kim K, Zolton JR, Perkins NJ, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a single anovulatory cycle detected by transvaginal sonography among 150 cycles in 53 women" Section: Interpretation.

facts.claim: LH surge or luteal progesterone assessment may overestimate true anovulation by including suboptimal ovulation, and ovulatory quality may exist on a continuum.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study
source.authors: DeVilbiss EA, Stanford JB, Mumford SL, Sjaarda LA, Kim K, Zolton JR, Perkins NJ, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "LH surge or luteal progesterone assessment may overestimate the incidence of true anovulation by including suboptimal ovulation" Section: Interpretation.

facts.claim: Time to pregnancy can be improved by either timing intercourse to the fertile window using biomarkers (LH, cervical fluid) or by increasing the frequency of unscheduled intercourse.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study
source.authors: DeVilbiss EA, Stanford JB, Mumford SL, Sjaarda LA, Kim K, Zolton JR, Perkins NJ, Schisterman EF

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "biomarkers or cervical fluid may help time intercourse to the fertile window, time to pregnancy can also be improved through increasing the frequency" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: In a simulation of 100,000 women over 12 cycles, reducing per-cycle anovulation incidence from 14.5% to 1% raised the cumulative pregnancy rate by only 4.0% (86.7% vs 90.7%).

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a reduction in the per-cycle incidence of anovulation from 14.5% to 1% resulted in a 4.0% higher cumulative pregnancy rate (86.7 versus 90.7%)" Section: Results/Abstract.

facts.claim: Increasing unscheduled intercourse frequency from weekly to every other day produced a 5-cycle median reduction in time to pregnancy (7 cycles to 2 cycles) in the simulation.

source.title: Sporadic anovulation is not an important determinant of becoming pregnant and  time to pregnancy among eumenorrheic women: A simulation study

facts.claim: Cumulative 12-cycle pregnancy rates in the simulation were 59.9% for weekly intercourse, 88.8% for every other day, and 91.6% for daily intercourse, a 28.9% absolute increase from weekly to every other day.
